banco_oraciones.oracion: There are three boxes on the table.
The children are in the garden.
My feet are tired after the walk.
There aren't any watches in the box.
The children aren't at school today.
How many children are there in the class?
Are there many people at the market?
Are the children happy? Yes, they are.
Are your feet cold? No, they aren't.
Are there two men at the door? Yes, there are.

interaccion: Sofía: Moving day! There are ten **boxes**: five with **books**, five with... more **books**.
Ben: My **feet** hurt. And I dropped a box on my **teeth**. Don't ask.
Kenji: In Japanese, one book and ten books are the same word. English has rules... and then it has **children**, **men** and **women**.
Abuela Carmen: And **people**, my dear. One person, two **people**. English is like my recipes: rules first, exceptions forever.
inicio.definicion_pragmatica: Plurals say there is more than one — most nouns add -s or -es, but the most common words in English break the rules: children, people, feet.

filas: child → children, foot → feet, tooth → teeth
man → men, woman → women, person → people

ejercicios.afirmacion: 'City' becomes 'cities' because it ends in consonant + y.

ejercicios.texto: My ___ (foot) are tired after the walk.
There are three ___ (watch) in the box.

ejercicios.respuesta: The children are in the garden
Are there many people at the market

ejercicios.texto_con_error: There are many persons at the party.
ejercicios.respuesta: There are many people at the party.

ejercicios.oracion_base: There is a box on the table.
ejercicios.respuesta: There are three boxes on the table.

ejercicios.oracion_base: The children are at school today.
ejercicios.respuesta: The children aren't at school today.

ejercicios.oracion_base: There aren't any watches in the box.
ejercicios.respuesta: There are some watches in the box.

ejercicios.pregunta: Are there many people in your family? (short answer)
Do fish have feet? (short answer, No)

guion: Sofía: OK, moving plan. How many boxes are there?
Ben: Ten boxes. Five are full of books.
Ben: More books. And two watches, three caps... and a fish.
Ben: Don't worry! Fish don't need many things. He has his box... with water, obviously.

comprension.pregunta: What surprise is in the boxes?

reading.texto: Market day in the neighborhood. There are people everywhere: men with boxes of mangoes, women with baskets, children running between the tables. Abuela Carmen knows everybody. 'Those two men sell the best fish,' she says. 'And that woman has cherries from her own trees.' Kenji counts in his notebook: fifteen families, six dogs, hundreds of apples, and one grandmother who knows the names of all the people — and the prices of all the things.

preguntas.afirmacion: There are many people at the market.
Abuela Carmen doesn't know anybody at the market.
